metric_value: Multi-tiered server-centric architecture: browser -> Web Server -> Pilot Internet Publisher -> Pilot Analysis Server -> MDB
notes: Publisher acts as ISAPI service translating queries to multidimensional requests

metric_value: Pilot rejected standard CGI in favor of Microsoft ISAPI for faster and more secure query responses
notes: Key technical differentiator; faster query response through ISAPI on IIS

metric_value: AT&T UNIX SVR4, Digital UNIX, HP UX, IBM AIX, Sequent DYNIX, Sun Solaris
notes: Analysis Server ran on NT and 6 UNIX variants

metric_value: Native connections to Oracle and Sybase; ODBC to SQL Server / Informix / IBM DB2
notes: Broad RDBMS support

metric_value: $10000 per Windows NT Server plus $695/user
notes: Pricing as published February 1997

metric_value: $20000 per Windows NT Server or $30000 per UNIX server
notes: UNIX premium of $10000 over NT

metric_value: 350 employees worldwide
notes: As of profile date (early 1997)

metric_value: $1.5 billion
notes: Cognizant revenue disclosed in profile; Pilot was subsidiary at that time

metric_value: Three areas: reduced maintenance/installation; cost avoidance (no fat-client); desktop platform independence
notes: Aberdeen framework for web-OLAP economic justification

metric_value: Comprehensive suite: Internet Publisher + Analysis Server + Discovery Server (data mining) + Desktop + Designer + Sales & Marketing Library + Excel Add-In
notes: Full suite breadth as of 1997

metric_value: Specifically tuned for retail and financial services — promotional effectiveness and risk management
notes: Aberdeen notes Pilot's vertical specialization

metric_value: Stateless model enabling advanced browser features: move/resize/tile windows and bookmarking
notes: Stateless design as competitive differentiator

metric_value: Distribution of personal cubes (exported MDB subsets) via email as compressed self-extracting files for offline analysis
notes: Partnership with Blue Isle Software for personal cube delivery

metric_value: Exceptions analysis (color-coded); Ranking analysis (ascending/descending); 80/20 analysis (Pareto segmentation) — all as source code modules
notes: Three prebuilt analytical methods shipped as source code

metric_value: Aberdeen predicts Pilot will continue to offer a solid cost-effective choice for distributed OLAP through upcoming months and years
notes: Forward-looking Aberdeen endorsement

metric_value: Web-driven functionality will extend OLAP to more users than enterprises have been able to cost-justify in the past
notes: Prediction that web delivery model would democratize OLAP adoption

metric_value: Pilot Software acquired by SAP AG in February 2007; product rebranded SAP Strategy Management
notes: Sources: Wikipedia (Pilot Software); company changed hands multiple times: Cognizant -> Platinum Equity (1997) -> Accrue Software (2000) -> MBO (2002) -> SAP (2007)

metric_value: Microsoft OLAP Services (1999) and later SQL Server Analysis Services became dominant web-OLAP platform; Pilot's web-OLAP approach was commercially validated but market consolidated to Microsoft/Oracle/IBM
notes: Aberdeen's prediction of web-OLAP adoption proved highly accurate; vendor prediction mixed

metric_value: Pilot planned mid-1997 delivery of advanced Selector Object using Java and ActiveX for hierarchical dimension navigation
notes: Forward-looking product roadmap item

metric_value: Integrates information and technology to create business insight; focuses on healthcare / media / high-tech growth markets
notes: Cognizant split into IMS Health and Nielsen Media Research in 1998; Pilot sold off separately

metric_value: For enterprises needing to support large populations with multidimensional data access, combination of Pilot Internet Publisher and Pilot Analysis Server delivers complete Web-enabled OLAP solution
notes: Strong Aberdeen endorsement

metric_value: Multidimensional Database (MDB) organizes data in typical business terms: revenues/costs/gross margins vs. customers/products/regions/time
notes: Core technical architectural element

metric_value: Adding new user requires only browser and network access — not fat-client software installation — enabling large-population deployment cost-justification
notes: Economic argument for web-OLAP over traditional client-server OLAP
